data.content:
### Run Toolbox CLI (Python)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Executes the `toolbox.py` script using the Python interpreter. This command is used to start the PostHog toolbox CLI, optionally with flags to customize behavior like claim duration.

```bash
python toolbox.py [flags]
```

--------------------------------

### PostHog CLI Help Command

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/cli/README.md

Displays the main help information for the PostHog CLI, listing available commands and global options. This is the entry point for understanding the CLI's capabilities.

```bash
> posthog-cli --help
The command line interface for PostHog 🦔

Usage: posthog-cli [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  login      Interactively authenticate with PostHog, storing a personal API token locally. You can also use the environment variables `POSTHOG_CLI_TOKEN`, `POSTHOG_CLI_ENV_ID` and `POSTHOG_CLI_HOST`
  query      Run a SQL query against any data you have in posthog. This is mostly for fun, and subject to change
  sourcemap  Upload a directory of bundled chunks to PostHog
  help       Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
      --host <HOST>  The PostHog host to connect to [default: https://us.posthog.com]
  -h, --help         Print help
  -V, --version      Print version

```

--------------------------------

### Example AWS CLI Command for S3 Data Deletion

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/capture/docs/llma-capture-overview.md

This snippet demonstrates how to use the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI) to recursively delete all objects within a specified S3 bucket prefix. This is useful for removing all data associated with a particular team.

```bash
aws s3 rm s3://posthog-llm-analytics/llma/123/ --recursive
```

--------------------------------

### Run Toolbox CLI Directly (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Executes the `toolbox.py` script directly on Unix-based systems after making it executable. This is an alternative to running it via the `python` interpreter.

```bash
./toolbox.py [flags]
```

--------------------------------

### PostHog CLI Environment Variables for Authentication

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/cli/README.md

Defines the environment variables used for authenticating the PostHog CLI in CI/CD pipelines. These variables allow non-interactive authentication by specifying the host, API token, and environment ID.

```bash
# POSTHOG_CLI_HOST: The PostHog host to connect to [default: https://us.posthog.com]
# POSTHOG_CLI_TOKEN: [A posthog person API key.](https://posthog.com/docs/api#private-endpoint-authentication)
# POSTHOG_CLI_ENV_ID: The ID number of the project/environment to connect to. E.g. the "2" in `https://us.posthog.com/project/2`

```

--------------------------------

### Make Script Executable (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

This command makes the `toolbox.py` script executable on Unix-based systems. It uses `chmod` to change file permissions, granting execute rights to the owner.

```bash
chmod +x toolbox.py
```

--------------------------------

### Connect to Pod (Custom Duration) (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Connects to a PostHog toolbox pod, specifying a custom claim duration in hours using the `--claim-duration` flag. This allows for shorter or longer sessions than the default.

```bash
python toolbox.py --claim-duration 4
```

--------------------------------

### Configure S3 Lifecycle Rules with AWS CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/S3_QUERY_CACHE_SETUP.md

This snippet configures S3 lifecycle rules for automatic deletion of query cache objects based on the `ttl_days` tag. It requires a JSON file defining the rules and the `aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration` command. Ensure you replace `your-bucket-name` with your actual S3 bucket name.

```bash
# Create rules for common TTL values: 1, 2, 7, 14, 30 days
cat > lifecycle-config.json << EOF
{
    "Rules": [
        {
            "ID": "query-cache-ttl-1-day",
            "Status": "Enabled",
            "Filter": {
                "And": {
                    "Tags": [
                        {"Key": "ttl_days", "Value": "1"},
                    ]
                }
            },
            "Expiration": {"Days": 1}
        },
        {
            "ID": "query-cache-ttl-7-days",
            "Status": "Enabled",
            "Filter": {
                "And": {
                    "Tags": [
                        {"Key": "ttl_days", "Value": "7"},
                    ]
                }
            },
            "Expiration": {"Days": 7}
        }
    ]
}
EOF

aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration \
    --bucket your-bucket-name \
    --lifecycle-configuration file://lifecycle-config.json
```

--------------------------------

### Update Pod Claim Duration (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Extends the duration of an existing PostHog toolbox pod claim using the `--update-claim` and `--claim-duration` flags. This is useful for continuing work on a pod beyond its initial claim period.

```bash
python toolbox.py --update-claim --claim-duration 24
```

--------------------------------

### Connect to Pod (Default Duration) (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Connects to a PostHog toolbox pod using the default claim duration of 12 hours. This is the simplest way to start a session with a toolbox pod.

```bash
python toolbox.py
```

--------------------------------

### Create Release Tag and Push - Bash

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/cli/CONTRIBUTING.md

This bash script demonstrates the process of creating a release tag for the posthog-cli application. It involves checking out a new branch, updating the version number in Cargo.toml, committing the changes, creating a Git tag, and pushing the branch and tags to the remote repository. It also includes an optional step to publish to crates.io.

```bash
git checkout -b "cli/release-v0.1.0-pre1"
# Bump version number in Cargo.toml and build to update Cargo.lock
git add .
git commit -m "Bump version number"
git tag "posthog-cli-v0.1.0-prerelease.1"
git push
git push --tags
# Optional - also publish to crates.io
cd cli && cargo publish
```

--------------------------------

### Bash: PostHog Worktree Command Reference

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

A quick reference guide for the 'phw' command-line tool used for managing PostHog worktrees. This lists all available commands with their basic syntax and purpose, serving as a cheat sheet for common operations like creation, switching, and removal.

```bash
phw create <branch> [base-branch]   # Create new branch & worktree (defaults to master)
phw checkout <branch>               # Create worktree for existing branch
phw switch <branch>                 # Switch to existing worktree
phw pr <number>                     # Checkout PR in worktree
phw remove <branch>                 # Remove worktree
phw list                            # List all worktrees
```

--------------------------------

### Remove Xcode Command Line Tools (macOS Sonoma)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Removes Xcode Command Line Tools to resolve a specific cstring compilation error on macOS Sonoma.

```bash
sudo rm -rf /Library/Developer/CommandLineTools
```

--------------------------------

### Bash: Debugging Flox and Git Worktrees

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Provides essential commands for debugging issues related to Flox environments and Git worktrees within the PostHog project. 'flox list' and 'flox search --installed' help inspect the current Flox environment, while 'git worktree list' confirms the status of Git worktrees.

```bash
# Check Flox environment status
flox list

# See what's installed in current environment
flox search --installed

# Check current worktrees
git worktree list
```

--------------------------------

### Bash: Create, Checkout, and Switch PostHog Worktrees

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

This snippet demonstrates the core 'phw' commands for managing PostHog worktrees. It shows how to create new worktrees for specific tasks or branches, check out existing branches into worktrees, and switch between them. These commands facilitate isolated development for features, bug fixes, and PR reviews.

```bash
# Create isolated worktrees for different tasks
phw create haacked/feature-analytics
phw checkout bugfix/login-issue
phw pr 1234

# Work on feature
phw switch haacked/feature-analytics
bin/start  # Work on feature

# Stop and switch to bug fix
# Ctrl+C to stop
phw switch bugfix/login-issue
bin/start  # Switch to bug fix work

# Stop and switch to PR review
# Ctrl+C to stop
phw switch pr-1234-teammate
bin/start  # Review PR
```

--------------------------------

### Deploy PostHog Hobby Instance with Docker

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/README.md

This bash command downloads and executes a script to deploy a PostHog hobby instance using Docker on a Linux system. It requires at least 4GB of memory. The script fetches the latest deployment script from the official PostHog GitHub repository.

```bash
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/posthog/posthog/HEAD/bin/deploy-hobby)"
```

--------------------------------

### Run CI Evals with Pytest

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/ee/hogai/eval/README.md

This command executes all AI evaluations within the CI environment using pytest. It relies on the BRAINTRUST_API_KEY environment variable and specific pytest configuration files.

```bash
pytest ee/hogai/eval/ci
```

--------------------------------

### Review PostHog Pull Request in Isolated Worktree (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Uses the `phw pr` command to automatically fetch a specified pull request, create a dedicated Git worktree for it, and activate the associated Flox environment. This facilitates easy testing and review of PRs.

```bash
phw pr 12345
# Fetched PR, switched to worktree, ready to test!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000
```

--------------------------------

### Bash: Git Worktree Management Commands

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

This snippet includes general Git commands relevant to worktree management, complementing the 'phw' tool. 'git worktree prune' is used to clean up any stale or broken worktree references, ensuring the Git repository remains consistent. 'git worktree list' provides a low-level view of all active worktrees.

```bash
# Clean up unused worktrees
git worktree prune

# See current worktrees
phw list
```

--------------------------------

### One-Time Setup for PostHog Development Workflow (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Installs necessary tools (direnv, gh, jq), configures the shell to use direnv for automatic environment loading, and sources the `phw` helper script for auto-cd functionality. Finally, it activates the Flox environment in the main PostHog repository.

```bash
# Install dependencies
brew install direnv gh jq

# Add direnv hook to your shell (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"  # or bash

# Add the phw function for auto-cd functionality
echo 'source ~/dev/posthog/posthog/bin/phw' >> ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc

# Reload your shell
source ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc

# Verify setup in main repo
cd ~/dev/posthog/posthog
flox activate
# You should see Flox environment activate and uv sync run
```

--------------------------------

### Source phw Script for Command Usage

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Ensures that the 'phw' command is available in the current shell session. It also provides instructions on how to add it permanently to the Zsh configuration.

```bash
# Make sure you've sourced the phw script
source ~/dev/posthog/posthog/bin/phw

# Add it permanently to your shell profile
echo 'source ~/dev/posthog/posthog/bin/phw' >> ~/.zshrc
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies (macOS)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Installs necessary libraries for developing `hogql-parser` on macOS using Homebrew.

```bash
brew install boost antlr4-cpp-runtime
```

--------------------------------

### Run Backend Tests and Type Checking

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/wiki/development-process/Development-process

This command executes all backend tests and performs type checking. It is the standard procedure for ensuring backend code quality and correctness before merging changes.

```bash
bin/tests
```

--------------------------------

### List and Remove PostHog Worktrees (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Provides commands `phw list` to display all managed PostHog worktrees and `phw remove` to delete a specific worktree. These commands are designed to work across different `POSTHOG_WORKTREE_BASE` locations, leveraging Git's native worktree tracking.

```bash
phw list                                # See all worktrees
phw remove pr-5678-teammate            # Done with PR review
```

--------------------------------

### Execute PostHog LLMA Integration Tests with Cargo

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/capture/docs/llma-integration-test-suite.md

Provides commands to run integration tests for the LLMA functionality using Cargo, the Rust package manager. Supports running all tests, specific phases, tests with verbose output, and sequential execution.

```bash
# Run all LLMA integration tests
cargo test --test llma_integration

# Run specific phase tests
cargo test --test llma_integration phase_01
cargo test --test llma_integration phase_02

# Run with detailed output
cargo test --test llma_integration -- --nocapture

# Run tests in sequence (important for phase gating)
cargo test --test llma_integration -- --test-threads=1
```

--------------------------------

### Build and Run PostHog with Docker Compose

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/wiki/Deployment

These bash commands set up a local PostHog environment using Docker Compose on Ubuntu/Mac. It involves cloning the repository, building the Docker images, and then starting the services in detached mode. This method is suitable for local development and testing.

```bash
sudo apt-get install git
git clone https://github.com/posthog/posthog.git
cd posthog
docker-compose build
docker-compose up -d
```

--------------------------------

### Run Local Tests (macOS)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Executes the tests for the `hogql` module using pytest after installing the locally-built `hogql_parser` on macOS.

```bash
pytest posthog/hogql/
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies (Ubuntu)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Provides a link to a GitHub Gist for installing Antlr runtime on Ubuntu, as the default package might be an older version causing compilation issues.

```bash
In that case run commands from [this step](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/4fba6a63e351131fdb27b85e7ba436446fdb3093/.github/actions/run-backend-tests/action.yml#L100).
```

--------------------------------

### Install hogql_parser Locally (macOS)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Installs the `hogql_parser` package by building it from local source code on macOS.

```bash
pip install ./common/hogql_parser
```

--------------------------------

### Run PostHog Source-Specific Tests with Snapshot Update

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/products/revenue_analytics/backend/views/README.md

This command runs source-specific tests for the PostHog revenue analytics, including 'events' and 'stripe' sources. The `-v` flag enables verbose output, and `--snapshot-update` is used to update existing snapshots or create new ones if they don't exist.

```bash
# Test all events source builders
pytest products/revenue_analytics/backend/views/sources/test/events/ -v --snapshot-update

# Test all Stripe source builders
pytest products/revenue_analytics/backend/views/sources/test/stripe/ -v --snapshot-update

```

--------------------------------

### Install ANTLR on macOS

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/hogql/grammar/README.md

Installs the ANTLR binary on macOS using Homebrew. Ensure you are using a compatible version, as specified in the project's CI configuration if a newer version causes issues.

```bash
brew install antlr
```

--------------------------------

### Switch to Existing PostHog Worktree (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Employs the `phw switch` command to navigate to an already established Git worktree. This command activates the Flox environment for the target worktree, allowing immediate continuation of development.

```bash
phw switch haacked/new-feature
# Switches to already created worktree, Flox activated!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000
```

--------------------------------

### Seed Demo Data for PostHog Development Environment

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/README.md

These commands are used to populate the PostHog development environment with demo data. They are executed from the root directory of the 'posthog' repository. The first command generates general demo data, while the second sets up the specific testing environment.

```bash
# from repo root
$ ./manage.py generate_demo_data
$ ./manage.py setup_test_environment
```

--------------------------------

### Install Benchmark Tools (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/ee/benchmarks/README.md

Installs necessary tools for running benchmarks, including airspeed velocity and virtualenv. This is a prerequisite for local benchmark execution.

```bash
pip install asv virtualenv
```

--------------------------------

### Manage Interactive Flox Prompt Behavior

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/FLOX_MULTI_INSTANCE_WORKFLOW.md

Provides options for handling the interactive Flox prompt that appears when switching directories. Users can choose to skip nesting, allow nesting, or use 'phw' commands to avoid the prompt.

```bash
# Option 1: Skip nesting (recommended)
# Press Enter or 'n' when prompted, then:
exit  # Leave current environment
phw switch your-branch  # Switch cleanly and consistently

# Option 2: Allow nesting (if you prefer)
# Type 'y' when prompted, then remember to exit multiple times later

# Option 3: Use phw commands (avoids the prompt)
phw checkout your-branch  # Automatically handles switching
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific Backend Test Subset

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/wiki/development-process/Development-process

This command runs a specific subset of backend tests, identified by a module path. This is useful for targeted testing during development or debugging.

```bash
bin/tests posthog.api.test.test_action
```

--------------------------------

### Run Playwright Tests Against Local PostHog Instance

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/playwright/README.md

Command to run Playwright tests against a locally running PostHog instance. Requires setting environment variables for login credentials and the base URL.

```bash
LOGIN_USERNAME='my@email.address' LOGIN_PASSWORD="the-password" BASE_URL='http://localhost:8010' pnpm --filter=@posthog/playwright exec playwright test --ui

```
